name: 2020 Election Full
description: The '2020 Election Full' encompasses a range of unsubstantiated claims and conspiracy theories alleging widespread fraud and irregularities in the 2020 United States presidential election, which saw Joe Biden defeat Donald Trump. These theories generally assert that the election was 'stolen' or 'rigged' through various means, including manipulated voting machines, fraudulent ballots, and coordinated efforts by Democrats, foreign powers, and/or 'deep state' actors. Proponents often cite statistical anomalies, anecdotal accounts, and selectively interpreted data as evidence. These claims originated and spread rapidly through social media, right-wing news outlets, and political rallies following the election. Mainstream media outlets, election officials (both Republican and Democrat), and legal challenges have overwhelmingly refuted these claims. Multiple recounts, audits, and court cases have failed to produce credible evidence of widespread fraud that would have altered the election outcome. The Department of Justice and cybersecurity agencies have also found no evidence of widespread foreign interference that could have changed the election results. The persistence of these theories has contributed to political polarization and distrust in democratic institutions.
subtitle: Claims of widespread voter fraud.

claims.claim: Dominion Voting Systems machines were rigged to switch votes from Trump to Biden.
claims.evidence: Statistical anomalies in vote counts, allegations from individuals claiming to have witnessed manipulation, and selectively edited videos.
claims.criticism: Independent audits and recounts have consistently affirmed the accuracy of Dominion machines. Cybersecurity experts have debunked claims of widespread vulnerabilities. Lawsuits based on these claims have been dismissed for lack of evidence.

claims.claim: Massive numbers of fraudulent ballots were cast, particularly in key swing states.
claims.evidence: Affidavits from poll watchers alleging irregularities, statistical analyses claiming unusually high voter turnout, and anecdotal reports of dead people voting.
claims.criticism: Investigations by election officials and law enforcement have found only isolated instances of voter fraud, none of which were widespread enough to affect the election outcome. High voter turnout was largely attributed to increased participation and expanded voting options due to the COVID-19 pandemic.

claims.claim: Foreign interference, particularly from China or Venezuela, altered the election results.
claims.evidence: Unsubstantiated claims from individuals with alleged ties to intelligence agencies, selectively interpreted news reports, and conspiracy theories involving Dominion Voting Systems' ownership.
claims.criticism: U.S. intelligence agencies have found no evidence of foreign interference that altered the election results. Claims of foreign ownership or control of Dominion Voting Systems have been debunked.

keyFigures.name: Donald Trump
keyFigures.role: Promoted claims of election fraud and refused to concede the election.

keyFigures.name: Rudy Giuliani
keyFigures.role: Led legal challenges to the election results and promoted conspiracy theories.

keyFigures.name: Sidney Powell
keyFigures.role: Promoted outlandish conspiracy theories about Dominion Voting Systems and foreign interference.

keyFigures.name: Christopher Krebs
keyFigures.role: Director of the Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA), who affirmed the security of the election and was subsequently fired.

keyFigures.name: Bill Barr
keyFigures.role: Attorney General who stated the DOJ found no widespread fraud.

mythologyConnections: The 'Big Lie' trope resonates with concepts of misinformation and deception found in various mythologies.
The idea of a 'stolen' election echoes narratives of usurpation and rightful rulers being overthrown.

timeline.event: Major news outlets project Joe Biden as the winner.
Trump campaign launches numerous unsuccessful legal challenges.
Attack on the U.S. Capitol, fueled in part by election fraud claims.
Continued promotion and dissemination of election fraud theories.

sources.author: Robert P. Jones
sources.work: White Too Long: The Legacy of White Supremacy in American Christianity
sources.title: Robert P. Jones

sources.author: Zack Beauchamp
sources.work: Why Trump’s election denial is so dangerous
sources.title: Zack Beauchamp
